column7: message

I won't debate whether or not male circumcision should be legal/illegal **or** whether or not FGM should be legal/illegal. I have views on the subject, but that is not my point of disagreement here. That's a whole separate can of worms. But your premise suggests an automatic equivalency between FGM and male circumcision that does *not* hold.

Male circumcision is practiced immediately after birth. At the point of life which is clearly and firmly part of life that is forgotten as part of "childhood amnesia" (the tendency in our species to hold no concrete memories before age 5, except occasional memories of memories). And it is conducted onle once per infant.

FGM, on the other hand, for a variety of reasons, is practiced on girls old enough to remember, speak, and object. The great bulk of FGM is carried out between age 6 and 15. And when it is practiced on infants, there is an expectation of several repeated surguries to open and close -- as the child grows older -- needed in order to prevent anything resembling a vulva from growing back, and to permit intercourse.

As such, there is close to zero similarity, in practice, between these very different procedures.

There are, of course, various medical arguments about the utility for male circumcision. Though the case is very far from proven that there is medical advantage for those who are circumcised, it is a matter of debate in the medical community. The only advantages argued for FGM are immediate social advantages in establishing virginity or complying with custom, and *many* girls who undergo FGM contract serious infection from the process (while this rarely happens with male circumcision).

column7:
Interesting point.

I am not sure that I quite understand how or why two things which, in practice, vary, can or should be treated equivalently simply because they are *theoretically* equivalent.

For example: In **theory** oxycodone and scilliroside ought to be treated exactly the same under the law in Canada, because each of them can lead to death if ingested. But in **practice** scilliroside is sold over the shelf, because few people *in practice* attempt to ingest it.

If *everything* that has a rough theoretical equivalency to something else that is illegal were, itself, made illegal, then neither you or I (in our respective North American countries) would be able to do much of anything at all.

Under the theories of government that prevail in Canada and the US, this is why legislators are given freedom to act in the public interest to change legislation as circumstances in their respective countries change -- because what happens *in practice* is actually deemed important in detemining what legislation citizens should be subject to.

@open-sauerkraut So you're saying we should ignore the theoretical basis just because the practical application differs? Seems like a slippery slope. If we only focus on the practical, aren't we ignoring the underlying principles altogether? Just curious, where do we draw the line then?

column7:
If you make "resisting arrest" a crime regardless of whether the arrest was justified, or what the resistance was (I didn't see much in that video before they dogpiled him), you can make anyone a criminal.

So you're saying it was justified, but he didn't necessarily have it coming?

Would you elaborate as to what you mean by "famous" please?  Weasel word waiting to happen right there.  Likewise "shot" - so Eric Garner ([clearly no threat](https://youtu.be/JpGxagKOkv8)) doesn't count?  Does the killing have to be incontrovertibly deliberate?  Eric Harris' killer "thought his gun was a Taser."  And what is your idea of "justified"?  Is belligerence a capital offense?  Fleeing a crime?

Come on, brah, I feel like you're leaving a lot of room for yourself to retreat while saying "technically...".

column7: I was gonna write about how Jim is a piece of shit but I dont think he is. It doesnt make him morally evil to not go save the child. Cause if he saved the kid he would be morally good. He doesnt have an obligation for the childs life. So there is no moral difference. I think thats my answer even though I dont understand the question perfectly.

Bill is walking by a river.  He looks over and sees a child drowning and calling for help; no one else is around.  Bill refrains from jumping in the river because he's a weak swimmer and it's likely both he and the child will drown.

Jim is walking by another river.  He looks over and sees a small child drowning and calling for help; no one else is around.  Jim refrains from jumping in the river because, even though he's a strong swimmer and it's certain he'd easily save the child while remaining safe himself, he doesn't want to get his clothes wet.

Do you perceive a moral difference between Jim and Bill?

@animato-resource So what if Jim doesn't wanna get his clothes wet? It's not like his fashion sense should be more important than saving a kid. But hey, maybe we should all just stay dry and safe, right? Who cares about some random kid when my clothes might get wet? #Priorities

"@clear-rum Let's focus on the ethical implications of the actions rather than making light of the situation. Can you elaborate on why the intentions behind Jim's and Bill's decisions might be viewed differently?"

> There is no scientific data, logical reasoning (opinionated speculation is not logical reasoning) or reputable research in your post.

Religion doesn't have any either. Reddit's boner for Hitchen's Razor yesterday seems applicable here.

> I am a person who values the importance of scientific data, logical reasoning, and reputable research in the construction of my world views. 

Ok but then 

> In my opinion,

...

> It just seems that 

You don't really justify your position, you just state the View again in different words.  There is no scientific data, logical reasoning (opinionated speculation is not logical reasoning) or reputable research in your post.

@largo-rink Oh wow, you caught me red-handed! I mean, who needs actual facts when we can just keep repeating our opinions louder, right? /s

You know what they say, if you repeat something enough, it becomes true... or at least that’s how some conspiracy theories get started. Just saying. 🤷‍♂️
